questions.question: Explain the difference between a process and a thread. Why are threads preferred over processes for certain applications?
keyPoints: Process is an independent execution unit with separate address space
Thread is a lightweight execution unit sharing process resources
Threads have lower overhead for creation and context switching
Threads allow efficient communication via shared memory
Better for applications requiring concurrent activities like web servers

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Process Control Block (PCB)? List the information stored in a PCB.
keyPoints: PCB is the data structure representing a process in OS
Contains process state, program counter, CPU registers
Stores memory management info (page tables, segment tables)
Contains accounting information (CPU time used)
Stores I/O status information and list of open files

questions.question: Describe the five basic process states and explain how a process transitions between them.
keyPoints: New: Process being created
Ready: Waiting in queue for CPU assignment
Running: Instructions being executed by CPU
Waiting: Blocked waiting for I/O or event
Terminated: Execution completed
Transitions: New→Ready, Ready→Running, Running→Waiting, Waiting→Ready, Running→Ready (preempt), Running→Terminated

questions.question: What is inter-process communication (IPC)? Discuss two methods of IPC with their advantages.
keyPoints: IPC allows processes to communicate and synchronize
Message Passing: Processes exchange messages through OS
Shared Memory: Multiple processes access common memory region
Message Passing is simpler but slower due to copying
Shared Memory is faster but requires explicit synchronization
Other methods: Pipes, Signals, Sockets

questions.question: Explain the three types of schedulers in an operating system with their functions.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler: Selects processes from job pool to memory (admits new processes)
Short-term scheduler: Selects ready processes for CPU execution (CPU scheduler)
Medium-term scheduler: Swaps processes in/out of memory for degree of multiprogramming control
Long-term controls system utilization, short-term affects response time directly
Medium-term helps in managing memory and system load efficiently

questions.question: What is the critical section problem? List and explain the three requirements that a solution must satisfy.
keyPoints: Critical section is the part of a program accessing shared resources
Problem: Ensure only one process accesses critical section at a time
Mutual Exclusion: No two processes can be in critical section simultaneously
Progress: No process waiting to enter CS should prevent others from entering
Bounded Waiting: There is a limit on number of times other processes can enter CS after a process requests entry

questions.question: Differentiate between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling algorithms with examples.
keyPoints: Non-preemptive: Process runs until it voluntarily gives up CPU (e.g., FCFS, SJF non-preemptive)
Preemptive: OS can forcibly remove process from CPU (e.g., Round Robin, SJF preemptive)
Preemptive allows better response time for interactive systems
Preemptive can cause more context switches overhead
Round Robin uses time quantum for preemption

questions.question: Discuss the following CPU scheduling algorithms: FCFS, SJF, and Round Robin. For each algorithm, explain with an example and state its advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: FCFS: Processes scheduled in arrival order; simple but poor average waiting time; convoy effect
SJF: Shortest burst time first; optimal for average waiting time but causes starvation; difficult to know burst time
Round Robin: Time quantum based; fair for time-shared systems; performance depends on quantum size
Example calculations for each with waiting times
FCFS advantages: Simple, no starvation; disadvantages: High average waiting time
SJF advantages: Minimum average waiting time; disadvantages: Starvation, burst time prediction needed
RR advantages: Fair, good response time; disadvantages: Higher overhead, depends on quantum

questions.question: Explain the concept of multithreading. Discuss the different threading models (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many) and their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Multithreading allows multiple executions within a single process
Benefits: Responsiveness, resource sharing, economy, parallelism
Many-to-One: Multiple user threads to one kernel thread; simple but blocks entire process on system call
One-to-One: One user thread per kernel thread; allows true parallelism but limited scalability
Many-to-Many: Multiple user to multiple kernel; flexible but complex to implement
Thread libraries: Pthreads (POSIX), Win32 threads, Java threads

questions.question: What are the main issues in thread scheduling? Discuss multiple-processor scheduling and the challenges involved.
keyPoints: Thread scheduling: How OS assigns threads to processors
Single-processor: Similar to process scheduling with context switches
Multiple-processor: More complex with issues of load balancing and affinity
Processor affinity: Keeping process on same processor for cache benefits (soft affinity vs hard affinity
Load balancing: Distributing work evenly across processors
Symmetric (SMP): Each processor does its own scheduling
Asymmetric: One master processor controls scheduling
Challenges: Cache coherence, migration costs, NUMA effects
